index.heading: Ambitecture
index.text:
Ambitecture lets you design light and color in space, then perform it live.

Made by [Klaus Kobald](https://kobald.com) as an open source project.

Start here:
• [The stage](stage.index) — where you place and play your intents
• [Intents](intent.index) — the spatial "wishes" that shape the light
• [Scenes](scene.index) — saved looks you switch between

For real hardware (DMX, LED…) a matching [renderer](renderer) must be connected.

Tip: keep this panel's "Auto" toggle on and help will follow what you click. Turn it off for silence.

no-help-available.heading: Ooops!
no-help-available.text: No help is available for this topic.

renderer.heading: Renderer

heightSlider.heading: Height
heightSlider.text: Sets how high off the floor this element sits — the value it shows is the [Y (height) position](space.3d) in metres. On the stage you can also drag the small vertical handle next to it to change height directly.

space.3d.heading: Height & 3D space
space.3d.text:
The stage is your room seen from above, so you only see two of the three dimensions: X (left–right) and Z (near–far). The third is Y — height above the floor — which a top-down view can't show. Everything in Ambitecture, both [intents](intent.index) and [fixtures](fixture.edit), lives in real 3D metres and has a height.

You set height with the [height slider](heightSlider), or the small vertical handle on the stage; the number it shows is the Y position.

Why height matters:
• Reach is measured in full 3D. A light's [radius](intent.edit.radius) is a sphere around its position, so an intent with a 2 m radius sitting on the floor won't touch a fixture mounted 4 m up — the vertical gap alone is bigger than its reach. Raise the intent or widen the radius to include high fixtures.
• [Target](intent.index) intents aim moving heads in 3D: a moving head tilts toward the target's position, so the target's height decides whether beams point up, level, or down. Put a target at the height where you want the beams to meet.

stage.index.heading: The Stage
stage.index.text:
The stage is your room seen from above — a space where you place [intents](intent.index) (your spatial light "wishes") and see your fixtures. It works in three modes, shown as tabs:

• [Perform](stage.perform) — run the show live
• [Edit](stage.edit) — build and arrange your intents
• [Scenes](scene.index) — save and switch between looks

The hub takes the intents you place here and works out, for every physical fixture, what color and brightness it should show. New here? Read [what an intent is](intent.index).

stage.perform.heading: Perform
stage.perform.text:
Live mode. Here you play the show in real time: drag enabled intents around the room and tweak them with knobs, and the change goes out to your fixtures instantly.

Moves in Perform are temporary — they ride on top of the saved scene and don't change it. Use [Reset scene](stage.perform.resetScene) to snap back.

Controls: [HUD knobs toggle](stage.perform.hudToggle) · [knobs](stage.perform.knob). Only intents you marked in [Perform Enable](stage.edit.performEnable) can be moved here.

stage.perform.hudToggle.heading: Show / hide knobs
stage.perform.hudToggle.text: Shows or hides the round control knobs that float over each intent on the stage. Hide them for a clean view; show them to adjust intents live.

stage.perform.resetScene.heading: Reset scene
stage.perform.resetScene.text: Clears the live tweaks you've made in Perform and returns everything to the saved scene. Your scene on disk is never changed by performing — this just throws away the temporary changes on top of it.

stage.perform.knob.heading: Live knobs
stage.perform.knob.text:
Each knob adjusts one property of one intent in real time — brightness, radius, strobe, and so on. Turn it and the fixtures update immediately.

You choose which properties get a knob with the ❂ button in the intent editor ([quick panel](intent.edit.quickPanel)).

stage.edit.heading: Edit
stage.edit.text:
Build mode. Here you place intents, arrange them, and set their properties — changes are saved to the project.

In Edit the fixtures are locked and the physics is paused so nothing drifts while you work.

• Double-tap empty space to [add an intent](stage.edit.createIntent)
• Double-tap an intent to open its [editor](intent.edit)
• Double-tap a fixture to open its [fixture editor](fixture.edit)
• Drag an intent to move it

Toolbar: [Fixtures lock](stage.edit.fixtureLock) · [Perform Enable](stage.edit.performEnable) · [Perform sort](stage.edit.performSort) · [Select](stage.edit.select) · [Physics](stage.edit.physics).

stage.edit.fixtureLock.heading: Lock / unlock fixtures
stage.edit.fixtureLock.text: Fixtures are locked by default so you don't move them by accident while arranging intents. Unlock (🔓) to drag the physical fixtures into their real-world positions; lock again (🔒) when you're done.

stage.edit.performEnable.heading: Perform Enable
stage.edit.performEnable.text: Choose which intents the operator is allowed to grab and move during the live show. Tap an intent's circle to switch it on (blue) or off. Only enabled intents can be dragged or knob-controlled in Perform. Tap Done when finished.

stage.edit.performSort.heading: Perform sort
stage.edit.performSort.text: Sets the left-to-right order of the buttons on your Perform control strip. Drag the rows to reorder, then close — the new order is saved.

stage.edit.select.heading: Select
stage.edit.select.text: Tap intents to select one or several at once; their shared editor opens so you can change them together. Tap empty space to clear the selection.

stage.edit.physics.heading: Physics
stage.edit.physics.text: Turns the hub's physics solver on or off while editing. With physics on, intents linked by connections (rods, springs, ropes) pull on each other when you drag one. It's off by default in Edit so positions stay put; in Perform it's always on.

stage.edit.createIntent.heading: Add an intent
stage.edit.createIntent.text:
You added a new intent to the stage. Pick its type:

• Light — emits color and brightness around a point
• Master — a global dimmer / blackout over everything
• Target — a magnet that aims movable fixtures

The new intent joins the current scene. Double-tap it to open its [editor](intent.edit). See [intents](intent.index) for what each type does.

stage.edit.createFixture.heading: Add a fixture
stage.edit.createFixture.text:
With fixtures [unlocked](stage.edit.fixtureLock), double-tapping empty floor inside a zone places a new physical fixture there. Pick its profile from the list — each entry shows the profile's name and, in brackets, its class.

The fixture starts with just a name and position; its [editor](fixture.edit) opens right away so you can set the type-specific [parameters](fixture.edit.params), such as the DMX base channel. Double-tap outside every zone and nothing is placed — fixtures always belong to a zone.

fixture.edit.heading: Fixture editor
fixture.edit.text:
Double-tap a fixture on the stage in [Edit](stage.edit) to open this panel and adjust that physical fixture — its name, where it sits, and the settings specific to its type. Changes save to the project as you make them, and edits apply to this one fixture only.

Like intents, a fixture lives in [3D space](space.3d): its position has a height (Y) above the floor, set with the [height slider](heightSlider). Getting a fixture's height right matters — it decides whether a nearby light intent's radius actually reaches it, and where a moving head appears to be in the room.

The top fields are common to every fixture; below them a [Parameters](fixture.edit.params) section shows controls that belong only to this kind of fixture.

Fixtures come from profile files, not from this screen — and there is no editor yet to build a new fixture type from scratch. Ambitecture is open source, so a visual fixture builder is a great thing to contribute: see [the project](index).

fixture.edit.params.heading: Parameters
fixture.edit.params.text:
These controls are specific to this fixture's type. They come from the fixture's profile — a YAML file that describes what the fixture can do and which settings it exposes — so different fixtures show different parameters here (channels, ranges, options, and so on). Editing one changes only this fixture; the profile itself is a file you edit outside the app.

No profile for your hardware yet? Writing one — or building a proper in-app fixture editor — is a welcome open-source contribution. See [the project](index).

fixture.edit.delete.heading: Delete
fixture.edit.delete.text: Permanently removes this fixture from the project and from its zone. You'll be asked to confirm first — there is no undo. Any renderer driving the fixture stops outputting to it. To move a fixture instead of removing it, unlock fixtures and drag it, or edit its position fields.

intent.index.heading: What is an intent?
intent.index.text:
An intent is a "wish" for light at a place in the room — for example "warm amber glow here, about 5 metres wide". You never address a specific lamp; you describe what you want, and the hub works out what every nearby fixture should do to make it happen.

There are three types of intent:
• Light — emits color, brightness and spread around a position
• Master — a global brightness / blackout layered over everything
• Target — aims movable fixtures toward or away from a point

Every intent sits at a point in [3D space](space.3d), including a height off the floor — which matters for how far a light reaches and how a Target aims moving heads.

Place intents in [Edit](stage.edit), tune them in the [intent editor](intent.edit), and group them into [scenes](scene.index).

intent.edit.heading: Intent editor
intent.edit.text:
This panel edits the selected intent. Each row is one property with its value control; some rows have small buttons on the left to switch options.

• [Color](intent.edit.color) · [Radius](intent.edit.radius) · [Alpha](intent.edit.alpha) · [Blend](intent.edit.blend) · [Strobe](intent.edit.strobe)
• Row buttons: [On/Off](intent.edit.toggle) · [❂ quick knob](intent.edit.quickPanel) · [Shared/Overlay](intent.edit.overlay)
• [Connections](connections.index) link intents together

Top/footer: [× close](intent.edit.close) · [Copy](intent.edit.copy) · [Delete](intent.edit.delete).

intent.edit.close.heading: Close
intent.edit.close.text: Closes the editor and deselects the intent. Nothing is lost — your edits are already saved as you make them.

intent.edit.copy.heading: Copy
intent.edit.copy.text: Duplicates the selected intent (or intents) into the current scene, dropped slightly offset so you can see the copy. Handy for repeating a look across the room.

intent.edit.delete.heading: Delete
intent.edit.delete.text: Removes the selected intent. You'll be asked whether to remove it just from this scene (it still exists elsewhere) or delete it completely from the project.

intent.edit.toggle.heading: On / Off
intent.edit.toggle.text: Switches an optional property on or off. Off means "don't set this" — the intent leaves that property alone and other intents or defaults decide it. On sets it to a value you can then adjust. "Mix" appears when several selected intents disagree.

intent.edit.quickPanel.heading: ❂ Quick knob
intent.edit.quickPanel.text: Adds this property's knob to the Perform screen so you can adjust it live during the show. Tap again to remove the knob. Only some properties (like brightness, radius, strobe) can have a knob.

intent.edit.overlay.heading: Shared or Overlay
intent.edit.overlay.text: Decides whether this value belongs to the intent everywhere (Shared) or is an override just for the current scene (Overlay). Use Overlay when one scene needs, say, a redder color while every other scene keeps the shared one. "Mixed" appears when a multi-selection disagrees.

intent.edit.inputAssign.heading: Assign an input
intent.edit.inputAssign.text: Links a physical control — a button or a key — to this intent, so hardware can trigger it without touching the screen. (MIDI controllers are handled separately, in their own [plugin tab](plugin.index).)

intent.edit.performReset.heading: Perform reset
intent.edit.performReset.text: Picks which of this intent's values snap back to their saved setting when you clear your live Perform tweaks. Turn a value on here if a temporary change to it should be undone by Reset scene.

intent.edit.color.heading: Color
intent.edit.color.text:
Pick a color from the palette (hue, saturation, lightness). Behind the scenes the hub stores it in a device-independent form (CIE xyY — basically "which color" plus "how bright") and then maps it to whatever each fixture can actually produce, so the same color looks right across very different fixtures.

If several intents are selected, the sliders nudge every intent's color by the same amount instead of forcing them all to one color.

intent.edit.alpha.heading: Alpha (intensity)
intent.edit.alpha.text: How strongly this intent contributes — its overall intensity / opacity in the blend with other intents. Lower it to fade the intent back without changing its color.

intent.edit.blend.heading: Blend
intent.edit.blend.text: How this intent mixes with others that overlap it: Add (colors pile up and brighten), Alpha (normal layering by intensity), or Multiply (darkens / tints what's underneath).

intent.edit.radius.heading: Radius
intent.edit.radius.text: How far this intent reaches from its position, in metres. The radius is a sphere in [3D space](space.3d), so it counts height too: an intent with a 2 m radius on the floor won't touch a fixture mounted 4 m up, because the vertical gap alone is more than 2 m. Fixtures inside the sphere pick it up; the [falloff curve](intent.edit.radiusFunction) shapes how it fades toward the edge.

intent.edit.radiusFunction.heading: Falloff
intent.edit.radiusFunction.text: The curve that controls how the intent fades from full at the centre to nothing at the edge of its radius — a soft gradient or a harder edge.

intent.edit.strobe.heading: Strobe
intent.edit.strobe.text: Makes this intent flash. Higher values flash faster; zero is steady.

intent.edit.amber.heading: Amber
intent.edit.amber.text: An extra warm-amber channel for fixtures that have one, blended in on top of the main color for a warmer feel.

intent.edit.mass.heading: Mass
intent.edit.mass.text: How heavy the intent feels when dragged with physics on — heavier intents lag and swing more, lighter ones snap to your finger.

intent.edit.drag.heading: Drag
intent.edit.drag.text: How much the intent resists motion under physics — more drag settles it quickly, less drag lets it drift and overshoot.

intent.edit.position.heading: Position
intent.edit.position.text: Where the intent sits in the room in [3D](space.3d): X across, Y up (height), Z depth. You'll usually set X and Z by dragging on the stage, and Y with the [height slider](heightSlider), rather than typing.

intent.edit.freeze.heading: Freeze axis
intent.edit.freeze.text: Locks movement on the X, Y or Z axis so physics and dragging can't change that direction — useful to keep an intent on a line or at a fixed height.

intent.edit.layer.heading: Layer
intent.edit.layer.text: Stacking order for blending. Higher layers sit on top of lower ones when intents overlap.

intent.edit.name.heading: Name
intent.edit.name.text: A label for this intent so you can recognise it in lists and on the stage.

intent.edit.heightSlider.heading: Height handle
intent.edit.heightSlider.text: Turns on the little vertical handle on the stage so you can drag this intent's height — its [Y position](space.3d) — directly, instead of using the slider.

intent.edit.brightness.heading: Master brightness
intent.edit.brightness.text: The global dimmer. This Master intent scales the brightness of everything at once — pull it down to dim the whole room, to zero for blackout.

intent.edit.mode.heading: Attract / Repel
intent.edit.mode.text: For a Target: Attract pulls movable fixtures (moving heads) to aim toward this point; Repel pushes their aim away from it. Aiming happens in [3D](space.3d), so the target's height matters — set it with the [height slider](heightSlider) to tilt the beams up, level, or down.

intent.edit.followTime.heading: Follow time
intent.edit.followTime.text: How quickly fixtures swing to follow this Target. Short times snap; longer times glide smoothly.

connections.index.heading: Connections
connections.index.text: A connection physically links two intents so they move together under physics — drag one and the other follows. Choose the [kind](connections.kind) (rod, spring or rope) and tune its [strength](connections.param). Add one with + Connect, remove it with 🗑 ([release](connections.release)).

connectIntent.heading: Connect intents
connectIntent.text: Tap an intent on the stage to connect it to this one. Tap empty space, or × here, to cancel. See [connections](connections.index).

connections.kind.heading: Connection kind
connections.kind.text: Rod holds a fixed distance (stiff), Spring pulls back toward a rest length (bouncy), Rope only pulls when stretched taut (slack otherwise). Each gives a different feel when you drag linked intents.

connections.param.heading: Connection strength
connections.param.text: Tunes the linked behaviour — for example how stiff the rod's rotation is, or how strong the spring's pull is.

connections.release.heading: Release
connections.release.text: Removes this connection. The two intents move independently again.

scene.index.heading: Scenes
scene.index.text:
A scene is a saved look — a chosen set of intents with their settings. Switching scenes recalls that look instantly; only the active scene is sent to your fixtures.

A scene can also override specific values just for itself ([Overlay](intent.edit.overlay)) while sharing the rest.

• [Recall](scene.recall) a scene by tapping it
• [Rename](scene.rename) · [Copy](scene.copy) · [Delete](scene.delete)
• [Add/remove intents](scene.intentToggle) from a scene
• Trigger it from an [Input](scene.performInput) or a [Pulse Bucket](scene.performPulse)

To save the current live state instead, use a [snapshot](snapshot.index).

scene.recall.heading: Recall scene
scene.recall.text: Makes this scene active. Its look goes out to the fixtures right away, and any temporary Perform tweaks from the previous scene are cleared.

scene.rename.heading: Rename scene
scene.rename.text: Gives the active scene a new name so it's easy to find in the list and on your Perform buttons.

scene.copy.heading: Copy scene
scene.copy.text: Creates a new scene that starts as a duplicate of this one — a quick base for a variation without rebuilding from scratch.

scene.delete.heading: Delete scene
scene.delete.text: Removes this scene. You'll be asked to confirm; you can't delete the last remaining scene.

scene.intentToggle.heading: Intents in this scene
scene.intentToggle.text: Switches an intent in or out of this scene. A scene only sends the intents it includes, so use these toggles to decide what the look contains.

scene.performAssign.heading: Assign to recall
scene.performAssign.text:
The scene's Perform section lets you trigger this look without tapping it on screen — two ways:

• [Input](scene.performInput) — a button or a key
• [Pulse Bucket](scene.performPulse) — a beat clock that recalls it in time

scene.performInput.heading: Input
scene.performInput.text:
Links a physical input — a button or a keyboard key — to this scene, so pressing it recalls the look live without touching the screen. Tap the button to pick or change the input; assigning one makes it light up. (MIDI controllers don't go here — they live in their own [plugin tab](plugin.index).)

See also the other trigger: [Pulse Bucket](scene.performPulse).

scene.performPulse.heading: Pulse Bucket
scene.performPulse.text:
Puts this scene into a [pulse bucket](pulse.bucket) — a target a [pulse](pulse.index) drives — so the scene is recalled in time with the beat instead of by hand. Great for switching looks rhythmically with the music.

Tap the button to tick an existing bucket, or tap Create to make a new one (it's named and this scene is added to it). Assigning a bucket makes the button light up. The same bucket can also hold other scenes and animations that should change on the same beat. Each bucket row has a ✎ button to [rename it](pulse.bucket.edit) — a scene has nothing more to set; it's just recalled on the beat.

See also the other trigger: [Input](scene.performInput).

snapshot.index.heading: Snapshots
snapshot.index.text:
A snapshot captures the current live state — the active scene plus running pulses and animations — so you can jump straight back to it later. Unlike [scenes](scene.index), which you build, snapshots grab whatever is happening right now.

• [Create](snapshot.create) a snapshot
• [▶ Play](snapshot.play) to recall it
• [Modify](snapshot.modify) · [Delete](snapshot.delete)

snapshot.create.heading: Create snapshot
snapshot.create.text: Saves the current live state. Name it and choose what to capture — the scene, running pulses, and/or animations — then save.

snapshot.play.heading: Play snapshot
snapshot.play.text: Recalls this snapshot, restoring the captured state live.

snapshot.modify.heading: Modify snapshot
snapshot.modify.text: Rename a snapshot, change what it captures, or re-grab the current state into it.

snapshot.delete.heading: Delete snapshot
snapshot.delete.text: Removes this snapshot. The scenes and intents it referenced are untouched.

layout.presets.heading: Layout presets
layout.presets.text:
These buttons at the top switch how the screen is split into panes — one wide pane, a big stage with a column of smaller panes beside it, top-and-bottom, and so on.

Switching a preset only rearranges the panes; it never changes your scenes, intents or fixtures. Your choice and the sizes you drag are remembered on this device. Within each pane you pick what to show from its [tabs](pane.index).

pane.index.heading: Panes and tabs
pane.index.text:
The screen is divided into panes by the [layout preset](layout.presets) you pick. Each pane has a row of tabs along its top; tap one to choose what that pane shows:

• [Perform](stage.index) — run the show live on the stage
• [Edit](stage.edit) — place and arrange your intents
• [Scenes](scene.index) — saved looks you switch between
• [Control](control.index) — your live control board
• [Pulse](pulse.index) — beat clocks that drive movement
• [Animate](animation.index) — automatic motion and change
• [Snapshot](snapshot.index) — capture and recall the live state
• Plugin tabs — external [controllers](plugin.index) like MIDI

The same tab can appear in more than one pane; opening it just shows that view there.

control.index.heading: Control board
control.index.text:
Your live control board for the show — the buttons and controls you set up, gathered in one place so you don't hunt for them during a performance.

From here you can:
• [Recall scenes](control.scenes) with a tap
• Use [assigned triggers](control.assigns) — actions you mapped to buttons, keys or MIDI
• [Play and stop animations](control.animations)
• Set how scenes behave [on recall](control.autoReset)

It reflects what you built elsewhere: scenes come from the [Scenes](scene.index) tab, animations from [Animate](animation.index), and assignments from each intent's editor.

control.scenes.heading: Scene buttons
control.scenes.text: Each scene button recalls a saved [look](scene.index) — tap it and that scene goes live to your fixtures right away, clearing any temporary Perform tweaks from the previous one. The button order follows the order you set in [Perform sort](stage.edit.performSort).

control.assigns.heading: Assigned triggers
control.assigns.text: Controls here fire the actions you mapped to a physical input — a button or a keyboard key. You create these mappings in the intent editor ([Assign an input](intent.edit.inputAssign)) or when [assigning a scene](scene.performAssign); the control board just gives you an on-screen copy to press. (MIDI lives in its own [plugin tab](plugin.index), not here.)

control.animations.heading: Play animations
control.animations.text: Starts or stops the [animations](animation.index) you've set up, without leaving your control board. Playing one runs it live on top of the current scene; stopping it returns to the plain scene. Build and tune animations in the [Animate](animation.index) tab.

control.autoReset.heading: Reset on recall
control.autoReset.text: Decides whether recalling a scene also clears your live Perform tweaks automatically. On: every scene change starts clean from the saved look. Off: your temporary changes carry over until you clear them yourself with [Reset scene](stage.perform.resetScene).

pulse.index.heading: Pulses
pulse.index.text:
A pulse is a beat clock — a steady tempo that other things follow, so flashes, sweeps, scene changes and [animations](animation.index) all move together in time.

Each row is one pulse with its tempo in beats per minute. You can [play or stop](pulse.play) it, change its [speed](pulse.speed), and [edit](pulse.edit) its name and tempo. Make a new pulse with the [Create pulse](pulse.create) button at the bottom of this tab.

A pulse drives things through [pulse buckets](pulse.bucket) — named groups of scenes and animations that beat together. On its own a pulse does nothing visible; it only moves what's in a bucket on it. You can also [sync](pulse.sync) a pulse to an outside clock.

New to this? Make a pulse, then open a [scene's](scene.performPulse) or animation's Pulse Bucket button to put something on it.

pulse.create.heading: Create pulse
pulse.create.text: The Create pulse button at the bottom of the Pulse tab makes a new beat clock. Give it a name, then set its [tempo](pulse.speed) and [play](pulse.play) it. A fresh pulse beats but changes nothing until you put scenes or animations into a [pulse bucket](pulse.bucket) on it.

pulse.bucket.heading: Pulse buckets
pulse.bucket.text:
A pulse bucket is a named group — a target — that a [pulse](pulse.index) drives. One bucket can hold several things at once — scenes and animations together — so they all fire on the same beat. That's how a pulse makes more than one thing move in time.

Buckets are not created from the Pulse tab, which is why they're easy to lose. You make one from the thing you want it to drive:
• On a [scene](scene.index), tap its [Pulse Bucket](scene.performPulse) button.
• On an [animation](animation.index), tap its pulse button.

In the box that opens, tap Create and name the bucket — the scene or animation you started from is added automatically. To add more, open the Pulse Bucket button on another scene or animation and tick the same bucket. Each bucket row has a ✎ button for [more settings](pulse.bucket.edit), and untick (or 🗑) to take something out.

pulse.bucket.edit.heading: Edit a pulse target
pulse.bucket.edit.text:
The ✎ button on a bucket row opens its settings. Every bucket lets you rename it here.

What else you get depends on what the bucket drives:
• A [scene](scene.index) has no extra parameters — it's simply recalled on the beat, so there's nothing more to set.
• An [animation](animation.index) can be given trigger parameters — they decide how the pulse drives it, for example which command it fires on each beat, so the same animation can react in different ways. These only take effect when the animation is set to manual run mode; on auto run mode the animation runs itself and the parameters are ignored.

pulse.play.heading: Play / stop pulse
pulse.play.text: Starts the beat clock running (▶) or stops it (■). While it runs, everything in its [buckets](pulse.bucket) stays locked to the tempo; stopping it freezes those followers in place.

pulse.speed.heading: Pulse speed
pulse.speed.text: Sets how fast this pulse beats. Slide it up for a quicker tempo, down for a slower one — everything in the pulse's [buckets](pulse.bucket) speeds up or slows down with it. The current rate is shown in beats per minute (bpm).

pulse.edit.heading: Edit pulse
pulse.edit.text: Opens this pulse's settings so you can rename it and set its exact tempo. The name is what you'll see wherever you pick a pulse to drive a [bucket](pulse.bucket).

pulse.sync.heading: Sync to an outside clock
pulse.sync.text: Locks a pulse's tempo to an external clock instead of its own speed — handy for following a DJ or another device. You can choose whether the beat restarts on every bar, on every beat, or never, so it lines up cleanly with the music.

animation.index.heading: Animations
animation.index.text:
An animation makes something change over time on its own — a color drifting, a brightness breathing, intents sweeping across the room — so a look never sits still. Each animation is a small recipe of "keep doing this" that runs live.

Each row is one animation. From here you can [play, pause or stop](animation.play) it, [create](animation.create) a new one or reuse an existing one, and [edit](animation.edit) what it does. Several can run at once and stack on top of each other.

How animations fit with everything else:
• They run on top of the current [scene](scene.index) — they don't change the saved scene, and stopping them returns to the plain look.
• Aim one at particular lights by setting a [target](perform.intentFilter): tap intents on the stage.
• Give one musical timing by putting it in a [pulse bucket](pulse.bucket), so a [pulse](pulse.index) beat drives it instead of a fixed speed.

To freeze a running animation into a recallable look, take a [snapshot](snapshot.index).

animation.play.heading: Play / pause / stop
animation.play.text: ▶ starts the animation running live, ⏸ holds it on the current frame, and ■ stops it and returns to the plain [scene](scene.index). Resuming from pause carries on where it left off. While it runs it layers on top of whatever scene is active, so you can switch scenes underneath it.

animation.create.heading: Create or pick
animation.create.text: Create starts a fresh animation: you choose its type — the kind of change it makes (for example a drift, a sweep, or a breathe) — and then tune it. Pick existing reuses an animation already saved in the project, handy for running the same effect from more than one place. Either way it joins this list so you can play and adjust it. Set what it acts on with a [target](perform.intentFilter).

animation.edit.heading: Edit animation
animation.edit.text: Opens this animation's settings — what property it changes, how far it moves, and how fast. Each animation type shows its own controls. At the top you can trigger it from an [Input](animation.input) or drive it from a [Pulse Bucket](animation.pulse). If the animation is in a pulse bucket, its timing rides on that [pulse](pulse.index)'s beat instead of the fixed speed set here, so it stays locked to the music.

animation.input.heading: Input
animation.input.text:
Links a physical input — a button or a key — to this animation, so you can start and stop it from hardware during the show without touching the screen. Tap the button to pick or change the input; assigning one makes it light up. (MIDI controllers don't go here — they live in their own [plugin tab](plugin.index).)

See also the other trigger: [Pulse Bucket](animation.pulse).

animation.pulse.heading: Pulse Bucket
animation.pulse.text:
Puts this animation into a [pulse bucket](pulse.bucket) — a target a [pulse](pulse.index) drives — so its timing rides the beat instead of the animation's own speed. That's how you lock movement to the music.

Tap the button to tick an existing bucket, or tap Create to make a new one (it's named and this animation is added to it). Assigning a bucket makes the button light up. The same bucket can also hold scenes and other animations that should move on the same beat.

Each bucket row has a ✎ button for [more settings](pulse.bucket.edit): for an animation you can set trigger parameters there — how the pulse drives it on each beat — so the same animation can react in different ways (these apply in manual run mode).

See also the other trigger: [Input](animation.input).

plugin.index.heading: MIDI & controller plugins
plugin.index.text:
A plugin tab embeds an external controller — for example a MIDI surface — inside Ambitecture, so you can drive the show from hardware without leaving this screen.

Important: the controller doesn't act on its own. First tap an intent on the stage to define the target — what the knobs and faders, and any animation they drive, will affect. The chip shows the current target ([intent filter](perform.intentFilter)); tap another intent to change it, or clear it to start over. Until you pick a target, turning a knob does nothing.

The plugin runs as its own program elsewhere and appears here when it's reachable; if it isn't you'll see an [offline notice](plugin.offline). Fixtures and intents always live in the hub — a plugin only sends controls, it never owns your show.

plugin.offline.heading: Plugin offline
plugin.offline.text: This tab is showing a placeholder because its controller isn't reachable right now. Start that controller (or check it appears in discovery) and the panel will connect automatically — nothing in your project is affected meanwhile.

perform.intentFilter.heading: Pick a target
perform.intentFilter.text: Sets which intents this tab acts on. Single-tap a light on the stage to add or remove it as a target; the chip shows what's currently picked. Until you pick at least one, there's nothing for the controller or animation to drive. Clear it to act on all intents again. Use it to aim one animation or controller at just part of the room.
